title_en: Scholarly Discussions: Where an-Nawawi Disagrees with Others
title_ar: المناقشات العلمية: مواضع اختلاف النووي مع غيره
content_en:
An-Nawawi's scholarly personality combined deep respect for the tradition with a willingness to disagree — respectfully but clearly — when he found the evidence pointed elsewhere. The Sharh on Sahih Muslim contains numerous instances where he takes positions that differ from earlier scholars, sometimes including prominent Shafi'i authorities whose views one might expect him simply to transmit. These moments of disagreement reveal the standards he applied and the intellectual courage that characterized his best scholarship.

One consistent area of engagement concerns the interpretation of hadith texts where the apparent meaning conflicts with what an-Nawawi considers well-established legal or theological principle. Some earlier commentators resolved such conflicts by reinterpreting the hadith in ways an-Nawawi found forced or unsupported by Arabic usage. He regularly declines to follow these interpretations, preferring to acknowledge the tension openly and work through it more carefully, even when the result is less convenient.

His disagreements with the Qadi Iyad — the great Maliki scholar whose Ikmal al-Mu'lim was an important earlier commentary on Sahih Muslim — are particularly instructive. An-Nawawi respected Iyad's scholarship deeply and cited him throughout the Sharh. But he also found occasions where Iyad's legal formation led him to readings of a hadith that an-Nawawi thought were strained by the Maliki school's commitments. When this happened, an-Nawawi said so, explaining his reasoning and noting that the Shafi'i reading of the same text was more consistent with the Arabic.

On principles for resolving apparent contradictions between hadiths — what the hadith scholars called mukhtalaf al-hadith — an-Nawawi is especially careful to state his own methodology clearly before applying it. He recognizes four main approaches: reconciling the hadiths by showing they address different situations, determining which narration is stronger (tarjih) and preferring it, concluding that one narration abrogates another (naskh), or acknowledging genuine irresolvable tension and suspending judgment. His preference is always for reconciliation where possible, but he is honest about when reconciliation is artificial.

Among the areas where an-Nawawi shows independence is the treatment of narrators. While he is generally conservative in his judgments — reluctant to pronounce a narrator weak without strong evidence — he does not simply defer to earlier critics when he finds their reasoning inadequate. Where a narrator has been criticized on grounds that an-Nawawi considers insufficient, he says so and maintains the narration's reliability. Where he accepts a critical verdict, he explains what the evidence is and why it is convincing.

The character of his disagreements matters as much as their content. An-Nawawi never disparages scholars he differs with. His preferred formula is something like 'what the Imam said is well-known, but the stronger view in my estimation is' — language that acknowledges the authority of the person he is correcting while preserving the independence of his own judgment. This combination of deference and independence has made the Sharh a model of how scholarly disagreement should be conducted in Islamic learning.
